work_arrangement: I work remotely full-time (5 days/week from home)
home_situation: 2-bedroom condo with small patio in Long Beach
lifestyle_note: Avid podcast listener who dabbles in music production on weekends.
IQ1: My backyard is decent but I rarely use it during work hours. 2-bedroom condo with small patio in Long Beach I mostly see it through the window while I'm on calls. It feels like wasted potential honestly. I wish I could make better use of it.
IQ2: I need separation between work and home. Right now my desk is in the dining table and I can hear everything. When I'm on video calls it's embarrassing. I wish I had a real office.
IQ3: I've definitely thought about it — probably looked at those prefab office pods online a dozen times. The cost always held me back though. And I wasn't sure about permits in my area. It felt like a big commitment.
IQ4: I'd make it my dedicated work studio immediately. I'd soundproof it, add good lighting, and set up my equipment. It would transform my work-from-home experience. The mental separation of walking to a separate building would be huge.
IQ5: I struggle with boundaries honestly. Working from the dining table means I'm never really 'off.' I eat lunch at my desk, I check emails in the evening. Having a physical boundary like a separate structure would be life-changing.
IQ6: My immediate reaction is positive. The price point feels reasonable compared to contractors. One-day install means minimal disruption to my work schedule. My concern would be — can it handle SoCal heat? Does it have AC options? Also, what's the actual permit situation here?
IQ7: I'd need to know it's well-insulated and can handle triple-digit weather. Good electrical for my equipment is non-negotiable. If the HOA blocks it, that's a dealbreaker. And I'd want to see one in person before committing $23K.
IQ8: Sponsorship wouldn't really affect me either way. I discover home products through Reddit. Honestly, I'd probably find something like this through a targeted ad or a work-from-home forum. Word of mouth from someone who actually has one would be most convincing.
additional_thoughts: I've been waiting for a product like this. The ADU trend priced me out but this size and price make sense. I hope the quality matches the promise.

home_situation: 4-bedroom house with medium backyard in Pasadena
lifestyle_note: Weekend trail runner who coaches kids' soccer.

IQ3: I've definitely thought about it — probably looked at those prefab office pods online a dozen times. The cost always held me back though. And I wasn't sure about permits in my area. It felt like a big commitment.

work_arrangement: I work on-site / in-person full-time
home_situation: 3-bedroom townhome with tiny backyard in Pomona
lifestyle_note: Mountain biker who spends weekends at local trails and wrenches on bikes in the garage.
IQ1: My backyard is my staging area. 3-bedroom townhome with tiny backyard in Pomona I've got bikes leaning against the fence, a cooler always ready. It's where I prep for weekend rides. I love it but it's chaotic — my my partner would say it's a mess.
IQ2: Gear storage is my number one issue. My garage is packed and I can't fit my car in there anymore. I need a place to store bikes, boards, camping gear — all of it. A proper workshop area would be amazing too.
IQ3: I've looked at sheds from Home Depot but they feel flimsy. A real structure with power and lighting would be awesome. Cost and effort held me back — I don't want a months-long project. I just want something that works.
IQ4: Half workshop, half hangout. Pegboard for tools, rack for bikes, and a bench to sit and plan the next adventure. I'd probably spend more time in there than in the house. My my partner might actually appreciate that.
IQ5: I work work on-site full-time so the boundaries are pretty clear. I leave work at work and come home to my hobbies. I don't really need an office — I need a workshop. My garage is my current workspace for gear and it's overflowing.
IQ6: Interesting. $23K is real money but not crazy. One-day install is cool — I don't want a construction zone in my yard. My concern is whether 120 sq ft is big enough for what I need. Can I customize the interior? I'd want heavy-duty flooring and wall mounts.
IQ7: Price would need to come with financing. I'd want to see that it can handle real use — not just a pretty garden office. Heavy-duty flooring, ventilation for summer, and electrical outlets everywhere. Dealbreaker is if it feels too delicate.
IQ8: Outdoor event sponsorship would actually impress me. If I saw this at a mountain bike event or a trail running expo, I'd stop and look. That's way better than a random Instagram ad. I trust brands that show up in my world.
additional_thoughts: My dream is a backyard that's functional, not just pretty. If this product delivers on durability and customization, I'm interested. Just don't make it too precious to use.

work_arrangement: I am retired
home_situation: 3-bedroom ranch house with large backyard in Glendora
household: Married, empty nester, grandkids visit monthly
lifestyle_note: Passionate gardener who also does watercolor painting.
IQ1: We've got a large backyard. 3-bedroom ranch house with large backyard in Glendora It's fine but nothing special. I mow it and that's about it. I'd love to make better use of it but every project seems expensive. We've been putting things off.
IQ2: We need more room for the kids' stuff and my projects. I'm a crafting person and I have nowhere to do it properly. The the kids setup means every room is shared. A dedicated space would solve a lot of arguments.
IQ3: I've looked at Costco and Home Depot sheds but they're either flimsy or expensive. I thought about building something myself but I don't have the skills or time. The permit thing worried me too. I just want something simple that works.
IQ4: I'd use it for my DIY projects. Having a dedicated space where I can leave things set up without cleaning up every night would be incredible. The kids could play out there on rainy days too. Just needs to be sturdy and practical.
IQ5: The issue isn't work-life balance, it's life-life balance. With the kids, someone always needs the table, the couch, the TV. I am retired and when I'm home, there's no quiet spot. A backyard room would give us all more breathing room.
IQ6: $23K is a lot of money for us. That's not pocket change. But if it's genuinely installed in one day and doesn't need major permits, that's really appealing. My concern is whether we can afford it. Does it come with financing?
IQ7: Monthly payment option under $300. That's my threshold. I'd also need to know it can handle rough use — this isn't a museum piece. Dealbreaker is if it doesn't include setup and I'm stuck figuring out electrical and foundation.
IQ8: I don't pay attention to sponsorships much. I discover home products through YouTube reviews. Price and value drive my decisions. If a friend recommended it and I could see theirs, I'd be sold faster than any ad campaign.
additional_thoughts: I think the market for practical, affordable backyard structures is huge. Not everyone needs a fancy home office or yoga studio. Some of us just need more room. Keep the price competitive and the quality honest.

work_arrangement: I work on-site / in-person full-time
home_situation: 4-bedroom house with large backyard in Arcadia
lifestyle_note: Real estate hobbyist who follows property values closely and hosts backyard BBQs.
IQ1: I have a large backyard that's well-maintained. 4-bedroom house with large backyard in Arcadia I think about my property as an investment portfolio. Every improvement should add value. The backyard is underutilized square footage.
IQ2: I want to maximize my property's potential. Additional livable space — for guests, for rental income, or for aging parents — is my biggest need. The main house is maxed out and I don't want a full addition.
IQ3: I've had contractors out to quote an ADU. Minimum $80K, 4-6 months, full permits. It's a massive project. I've been waiting for a better option. Something that adds value without the headache.
IQ4: An income-generating space. I'd list it as a private backyard studio on Airbnb. Even at $75/night for weekends only, that's significant passive income. It pays for itself in under two years. That's the kind of math I like.
IQ5: Work boundaries aren't my concern — I work on-site full-time. I think about this purely from an investment angle. What adds the most value per dollar spent? A well-designed backyard structure is like adding a room without a major reno.
IQ6: Very interested. The price-to-value ratio is compelling. My questions are about durability, resale impact, and whether it qualifies for any tax benefits. One-day install is a major selling point. Permit-light is smart positioning.
IQ7: I need data on property value impact. Show me comps where a backyard structure added value. Build quality must be excellent — I won't put something on my property that looks cheap. HOA says no compliance is critical. Financing at reasonable rates would seal it.

work_arrangement: I work on-site / in-person full-time
home_situation: 2-bedroom condo with balcony and shared courtyard in Ontario
lifestyle_note: DIY enthusiast who watches home improvement shows and loves budget hacks.
IQ1: We've got a medium backyard. 2-bedroom condo with balcony and shared courtyard in Ontario It's fine but nothing special. I mow it and that's about it. I'd love to make better use of it but every project seems expensive. We've been putting things off.
IQ2: We need more room for the kids' stuff and my projects. I'm a creative person and I have nowhere to do it properly. The my partner setup means every room is shared. A dedicated space would solve a lot of arguments.
IQ3: I've looked at Costco and Home Depot sheds but they're either flimsy or expensive. I thought about building something myself but I don't have the skills or time. The permit thing worried me too. I just want something simple that works.
IQ4: I'd use it for my crafting projects. Having a dedicated space where I can leave things set up without cleaning up every night would be incredible. The kids could play out there on rainy days too. Just needs to be sturdy and practical.
IQ5: I work work on-site full-time so work boundaries aren't the main issue. Home boundaries are — finding personal space when you live with my partner. Everyone needs their own corner and our house doesn't have enough corners.
IQ6: $23K is a lot of money for us. That's not pocket change. But if it's genuinely installed in one day and doesn't need major permits, that's really appealing. My concern is whether we can afford it. Does it come with financing?
IQ7: Monthly payment option under $300. That's my threshold. I'd also need to know it can handle rough use — this isn't a museum piece. Dealbreaker is if it doesn't include setup and I'm stuck figuring out electrical and foundation.
IQ8: Sponsorship doesn't really matter to me. I find products through Instagram. Honestly, a neighbor having one would be the best marketing. If I could see it and touch it before buying, that would help a lot.
additional_thoughts: I think the market for practical, affordable backyard structures is huge. Not everyone needs a fancy home office or yoga studio. Some of us just need more room. Keep the price competitive and the quality honest.

work_arrangement: I work on-site / in-person full-time
home_situation: 2-bedroom apartment with shared backyard in El Monte
lifestyle_note: Musician who plays guitar and wants somewhere to practice without bothering neighbors.
IQ1: We've got a medium backyard. 2-bedroom apartment with shared backyard in El Monte It's fine but nothing special. I mow it and that's about it. I'd love to make better use of it but every project seems expensive. We've been putting things off.
IQ2: We need more room for the kids' stuff and my projects. I'm a DIY person and I have nowhere to do it properly. The my family setup means every room is shared. A dedicated space would solve a lot of arguments.
IQ3: I priced out a couple of shed options. The cheap ones look terrible and the nice ones are $15K+. I got sticker shock. I thought about a DIY approach but between my family and work, I have no time for a big project.
IQ4: A multipurpose room — craft space for me, play area for the kids, and overflow storage. Nothing fancy, just functional. Maybe a workbench along one wall and toy bins along the other. Practical and organized.
IQ5: The issue isn't work-life balance, it's life-life balance. With my family, someone always needs the table, the couch, the TV. I work on-site full-time and when I'm home, there's no quiet spot. A backyard room would give us all more breathing room.
IQ6: $23K is a lot of money for us. That's not pocket change. But if it's genuinely installed in one day and doesn't need major permits, that's really appealing. My concern is whether we can afford it. Does it come with financing?
IQ7: Financing is the make-or-break. If I could do $200-300/month, I'd seriously consider it. It needs to be durable — I don't want to pay $23K for something that falls apart in 5 years. And if my HOA might be an issue, that would stop me dead.
IQ8: I don't pay attention to sponsorships much. I discover home products through Instagram. Price and value drive my decisions. If a friend recommended it and I could see theirs, I'd be sold faster than any ad campaign.
additional_thoughts: I think the market for practical, affordable backyard structures is huge. Not everyone needs a fancy home office or yoga studio. Some of us just need more room. Keep the price competitive and the quality honest.

work_arrangement: I work on-site / in-person full-time
home_situation: 3-bedroom house with medium backyard in Walnut
lifestyle_note: Teacher who tutors on the side and wants a quiet space for evening tutoring sessions.
IQ1: We've got a medium backyard. 3-bedroom house with medium backyard in Walnut It's fine but nothing special. I mow it and that's about it. I'd love to make better use of it but every project seems expensive. We've been putting things off.
IQ2: We need more room for the kids' stuff and my projects. I'm a woodworking person and I have nowhere to do it properly. The the kids setup means every room is shared. A dedicated space would solve a lot of arguments.
IQ3: I priced out a couple of shed options. The cheap ones look terrible and the nice ones are $15K+. I got sticker shock. I thought about a DIY approach but between the kids and work, I have no time for a big project.
IQ4: I'd use it for my crafting projects. Having a dedicated space where I can leave things set up without cleaning up every night would be incredible. The kids could play out there on rainy days too. Just needs to be sturdy and practical.
IQ5: The issue isn't work-life balance, it's life-life balance. With the kids, someone always needs the table, the couch, the TV. I work on-site full-time and when I'm home, there's no quiet spot. A backyard room would give us all more breathing room.
IQ6: Honestly, the price makes me wince a bit. We could do a lot with $23K. But the one-day install and no-permit hassle are huge selling points. I'd need financing options for sure. What does the warranty look like?
IQ7: Financing is the make-or-break. If I could do $200-300/month, I'd seriously consider it. It needs to be durable — I don't want to pay $23K for something that falls apart in 5 years. And if my HOA says no, that would stop me dead.
IQ8: I don't pay attention to sponsorships much. I discover home products through Google searches. Price and value drive my decisions. If a friend recommended it and I could see theirs, I'd be sold faster than any ad campaign.
additional_thoughts: I think the market for practical, affordable backyard structures is huge. Not everyone needs a fancy home office or yoga studio. Some of us just need more room. Keep the price competitive and the quality honest.

work_arrangement: I am self-employed / freelance (primarily work from home)
home_situation: 4-bedroom house with large backyard in Claremont
lifestyle_note: Part-time consultant and potter who fires ceramics in the garage.
IQ1: I have a large backyard that my the kids enjoys on weekends. During the week it just sits there while I'm stuck inside working. I feel guilty not using it more. It could be so much more than just grass.
IQ2: I need separation between work and home. Right now my desk is in the guest bedroom and I can hear everything. When I'm on video calls it's embarrassing. I wish I had a real office.
IQ3: I've definitely thought about it — probably looked at those prefab office pods online a dozen times. The cost always held me back though. And I wasn't sure about permits in my area. It felt like a big commitment.
IQ4: A home office, no question. I'd set up my standing desk, dual monitors, and finally have a door I can close. The commute would be 30 seconds across the yard — dream scenario. My the kids would be thrilled to get their space back too.
IQ5: I try to keep boundaries but it's hard. My workspace is in the the kitchen counter so there's always overlap. I use headphones to signal 'I'm working' but it's not great. I don't have a dedicated workspace — more like a corner I've claimed.
IQ6: $23K is less than I expected for something like this! One-day installation is amazing — I was dreading weeks of construction. The permit-light part is clutch because that was my biggest worry. I'm genuinely excited but I want to know about insulation and soundproofing.
IQ7: Financing options would help — $23K is doable but not easy to drop all at once. I'd need good WiFi connectivity from the main house. If it felt flimsy or temporary, that would kill it. I want something that lasts 10+ years.
IQ8: I'm neutral on sponsorship but it wouldn't hurt. I find home products through Reddit. I'd trust a review from another remote worker more than any ad. A demo unit at a coworking event would be smart marketing.
additional_thoughts: I've been waiting for a product like this. The ADU trend priced me out but this size and price make sense. I hope the quality matches the promise.

lifestyle_note: Coach and fitness trainer who does personal training sessions and wants a private gym.
IQ1: I use my backyard constantly — stretching before runs, hosing off gear, hanging out after a ride. 3-bedroom house with medium backyard in Riverside It's functional but not organized. I feel like I need better storage solutions out there.
IQ2: Gear storage is my number one issue. My garage is packed and I can't fit my car in there anymore. I need a place to store bikes, boards, camping gear — all of it. A proper workshop area would be amazing too.

IQ7: Price would need to come with financing. I'd want to see that it can handle real use — not just a pretty garden office. Heavy-duty flooring, ventilation for summer, and electrical outlets everywhere. Dealbreaker is if it feels too delicate.
IQ8: Yeah, if they sponsored my local cycling club or a trail race, I'd notice. That shows they understand the customer. I discover products through friends and neighbors and word of mouth from my riding crew. A demo at an outdoor event would be smart.
additional_thoughts: I think there's a huge market for people like me who need gear space, not office space. Don't just market this as a home office — the adventure crowd needs storage solutions badly.
